1: ἄγε νῦν, οἱ πλούσιοι, κλαύσατε ὀλολύζοντες — ἄγε νῦν (come now) is James's rhetorical address marker (cf. James 4:13) that introduces a prophetic indictment. κλαύσατε (weep, aorist imperative) pairs with the participle ὀλολύζοντες (wailing/howling) — the second verb occurs in the LXX prophets specifically for eschatological lamentation over judgment coming on the wicked (Isaiah 13:6; James 14:31; James 15:3; James 23:1); James appropriates the prophetic genre directly. ταλαιπωρίαις (miseries) is hapax in the NT; the plural form intensifies the completeness of the coming suffering.
2: ὁ πλοῦτος ὑμῶν σέσηπεν, καὶ τὰ ἱμάτια ὑμῶν σητόβρωτα γέγονεν — both verbs are perfect tense: σέσηπεν (has rotted, from σήπω) and γέγονεν (has become). The perfects describe a completed state of decay that is prophetically proleptical — James speaks of future judgment as though already accomplished, a device common in OT prophecy (cf. Isaiah 40:1-2, where Babylon's fall is spoken as past). σητόβρωτα (moth-eaten) is a compound adjective; garments and stored grain/food represent the two primary forms of pre-monetary wealth storage in agrarian Palestinian society.
3: ὁ χρυσὸς ὑμῶν καὶ ὁ ἄργυρος κατίωται — κατίωται (has corroded/rusted, from κατιόομαι) is technically inaccurate (gold and silver do not rust), which is precisely the rhetorical point: the improbable corrosion of precious metals signals the completeness of the coming judgment. εἰς μαρτύριον ὑμῖν ἔσται (it will be as testimony against you) — the corroded wealth becomes a legal witness in the divine court. φάγεται τὰς σάρκας ὑμῶν ὡς πῦρ (will eat your flesh like fire) — the devouring imagery echoes the consuming fire of Malachi 4:1 and Isaiah 10:16-17.
4: ἰδοὺ ὁ μισθὸς τῶν ἐργατῶν... ὁ ἀπεστερημένος ἀφ' ὑμῶν κράζει — ἀπεστερημένος (defrauded, perfect passive participle) echoes the Septuagintal language of Leviticus 19:13 (οὐ μὴ ἀποστερήσεις) and Deuteronomy 24:14-15, the Mosaic law protecting day laborers' wages. The withheld wages κράζουσιν (cry out) — the same verb as Abel's blood crying out from the ground (Genesis 4:10 LXX: φωνὴ αἵματος... βοᾷ). The divine title κύριος σαβαώθ (Lord of Hosts/Sabaoth) is the only occurrence in the NT; by using the Hebrew transliteration rather than the Greek equivalent (κύριος παντοκράτωρ), James evokes the specifically prophetic tradition — the battle-Lord who vindicates the oppressed.
5: ἐτρυφήσατε ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς καὶ ἐσπαταλήσατε — the two aorists describe completed patterns of self-indulgence: τρυφάω (to live luxuriously, in soft indulgence) and σπαταλάω (to live wantonly/wastefully — the same verb used of the prodigal son in 1 Timothy 5:6 for widows who 'live for pleasure'). ἐθρέψατε τὰς καρδίας ὑμῶν ἐν ἡμέρᾳ σφαγῆς (you have fattened your hearts in a day of slaughter) — the image of livestock fattened for the day they will be killed is drawn from Jeremiah 12:3 LXX (ἁγίασον αὐτοὺς εἰς ἡμέραν σφαγῆς αὐτῶν); the rich who fatten themselves are unwittingly preparing themselves for divine slaughter.
6: κατεδικάσατε, ἐφονεύσατε τὸν δίκαιον — the judicial chain: κατεδικάσατε (you condemned, legal sentence) followed by ἐφονεύσατε (you murdered, execution of that sentence). τὸν δίκαιον (the righteous one) — the definite article with the singular may indicate a specific individual or function as a collective: 'the righteous person [characteristically].' Patristic readers saw an allusion to Christ (cf. Acts 3:14; 7:52 where Stephen uses the same title of Jesus), but the collective reading (the poor and righteous who are systematically oppressed) fits the social-justice argument James has been making. οὐκ ἀντιτάσσεται ὑμῖν (does not resist you) — the passive non-resistance of the righteous victim intensifies the moral culpability of the perpetrators.
7: μακροθυμήσατε οὖν, ἀδελφοί, ἕως τῆς παρουσίας τοῦ κυρίου — James shifts from prophetic indictment of the rich to direct pastoral address to the community. μακροθυμία (long-suffering, patience-toward-persons — as distinct from ὑπομονή, endurance of circumstances) is the quality of not retaliating or despairing when people treat you unjustly. παρουσία (coming, arrival — the standard NT eschatological term for the Lord's return) is James's sole use of the term; the agricultural illustration: ἐκδέχεται (eagerly awaits) + πρόϊμον καὶ ὄψιμον (early and late rain — the October-November planting rains and the March-April pre-harvest rains of Palestine, cf. Deuteronomy 11:14; Joel 2:23) — the farmer's patient waiting for the climatically necessary rains models the community's active, hope-grounded waiting for the parousia.
8: μακροθυμήσατε καὶ ὑμεῖς, στηρίξατε τὰς καρδίας ὑμῶν, ὅτι ἡ παρουσία τοῦ κυρίου ἤγγικεν — στηρίξατε τὰς καρδίας (strengthen your hearts) — the same verb Paul uses in 1 Thessalonians 3:13 ('establish your hearts unblameable in holiness') and Luke 22:32 ('strengthen your brothers'). ἤγγικεν (has drawn near, perfect of ἐγγίζω) — the same verb Jesus used to announce the kingdom's arrival (Mark 1:15; Matthew 4:17); the perfect tense indicates a present state resulting from a past movement: the parousia has come near and remains near. This nearness is the theological foundation for James's pastoral ethics throughout the letter.
9: μὴ στενάζετε κατ' ἀλλήλων, ἀδελφοί, ἵνα μὴ κριθῆτε — στενάζετε (groan, sigh — the present imperative prohibition) covers not just verbal complaint but the internal disposition of resentment and mutual grievance that pressure produces in a community. The prohibition is grounded eschatologically: ἵνα μὴ κριθῆτε (so that you will not be judged). ὁ κριτὴς πρὸ τῶν θυρῶν ἕστηκεν (the Judge stands before the doors) — the same vivid spatial metaphor as Revelation 3:20 (ἰδοὺ ἕστηκα ἐπὶ τὴν θύραν); the Judge standing at the threshold makes the eschatological boundary between 'now' and 'judgment' spatially present rather than temporally distant.
10: ὑπόδειγμα λάβετε, ἀδελφοί, τῆς κακοπαθίας καὶ τῆς μακροθυμίας τοὺς προφήτας — ὑπόδειγμα (example, model for imitation — as distinct from τύπος which carries typological/representative force) presents the prophets as directly imitable examples rather than typological prefigurations. κακοπαθίας (suffering, hardship-endurance) is paired with μακροθυμίας (patient endurance toward difficult persons) — the same pairing James used in James 5:7 for the farmer. τοὺς λαλήσαντας ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι κυρίου (those who spoke in the name of the Lord) — speaking in the Lord's name is precisely what made the prophets targets: Jeremiah, Amos, Isaiah all suffered for their authoritative proclamation.
11: ἰδοὺ μακαρίζομεν τοὺς ὑπομείναντας· τὴν ὑπομονὴν Ἰὼβ ἠκούσατε — James shifts from μακροθυμία to ὑπομονή (endurance of circumstances) in characterizing Job — a subtle distinction: Job's test was not primarily against difficult persons but against catastrophic loss. μακαρίζομεν (we call blessed, present tense — a community practice of beatitude-declaration). τὸ τέλος κυρίου εἴδετε — 'the Lord's telos' is ambiguous: either God's final purpose/resolution (the restoration of Job in James 42) or the outcome God brought about for Job. πολύσπλαγχνός ἐστιν ὁ κύριος καὶ οἰκτίρμων — πολύσπλαγχνος (very compassionate, rich in compassion) is a NT hapax and LXX hapax; οἰκτίρμων derives from the classic OT divine character formula (Exodus 34:6 LXX: οἰκτίρμων καὶ ἐλεήμων).
12: πρὸ πάντων δέ, ἀδελφοί μου, μὴ ὀμνύετε — πρὸ πάντων (above all, before everything) — this emphatic marker indicates the special gravity of the oath prohibition; it is not a new topic but the capstone of the patience-endurance section: community members under pressure are tempted to invoke oaths to give weight to their complaints. μὴ ὀμνύετε μήτε τὸν οὐρανὸν μήτε τὴν γῆν μήτε ἄλλον τινὰ ὅρκον (do not swear by heaven or earth or any other oath) — this broadens even Jesus's formulation in Matthew 5:34-36, which specifies particular forbidden oath-objects; James prohibits all oath-invocation. ἤτω δὲ ὑμῶν τὸ ναὶ ναὶ καὶ τὸ οὒ οὔ (let your yes be yes and your no be no) — the repeated single affirmations (ναὶ ναί, οὒ οὔ) indicate unambiguous simplicity, not doubling-up as an oath substitute.
13: κακοπαθεῖ τις ἐν ὑμῖν; προσευχέσθω· εὐθυμεῖ τις; ψαλλέτω — the three-question structure of James 5:13-14 covers the experiential range of community life: κακοπαθεῖ (is suffering/undergoing hardship — the same root as James 5:10's κακοπαθίας); εὐθυμεῖ (is in good cheer/cheerful — the same root as Acts 27:22, 25 where Paul encourages the sailors). The response to suffering is προσευχέσθω (let him pray, present imperative — continuous prayer); the response to cheerfulness is ψαλλέτω (let him sing psalms/praise, present imperative) — both responses are communally visible and orient the individual's state toward God.
14: ἀσθενεῖ τις ἐν ὑμῖν; προσκαλεσάσθω τοὺς πρεσβυτέρους τῆς ἐκκλησίας — ἀσθενεῖ (is sick/weak) encompasses both physical illness and the weakened condition that illness produces in relation to God and community. The sick person is to call the elders — the initiative lies with the sick person, not passively waiting for pastoral visit. τοὺς πρεσβυτέρους τῆς ἐκκλησίας (the elders of the church) — the plural indicates this is a communal act, not an individual rite; James presupposes an established church structure. ἀλείψαντες αὐτὸν ἐλαίῳ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ κυρίου — ἀλείψαντες (anointing, aorist participle) rather than χρίσαντες (anointing with ritual/ceremonial connotation); oil was a standard Greco-Roman and Jewish medical treatment (cf. Luke 10:34), but ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι (in the name of the Lord) locates the efficacy in the Lord's authority, not the medical application.
15: ἡ εὐχὴ τῆς πίστεως σώσει τὸν κάμνοντα — εὐχὴ τῆς πίστεως (the prayer of faith, genitive of quality) — faith characterizes the prayer, not the sick person or the elders specifically; the prayer is trust-saturated and directed to the Lord whose name was invoked in the anointing (James 5:14). σώσει (will save, future) — σῴζω covers both physical restoration and spiritual salvation; James likely intends both registers given the added clause about sin-forgiveness. ἐγερεῖ αὐτὸν ὁ κύριος (the Lord will raise him up) — ἐγείρω is the standard NT resurrection verb; its use here for physical recovery carries eschatological overtones. κἂν ἁμαρτίας ᾖ πεποιηκώς, ἀφεθήσεται αὐτῷ (even if he has committed sins, they will be forgiven him) — divine passive; the connection of illness and sin is not causal (contra a mechanistic health-and-wealth theology) but pastoral: the whole person — body and soul — is addressed in the rite.
16: ἐξομολογεῖσθε οὖν ἀλλήλοις τὰς ἁμαρτίας καὶ εὔχεσθε ὑπὲρ ἀλλήλων — the mutual structure is emphatic: ἀλλήλοις (to one another) and ὑπὲρ ἀλλήλων (for one another) — this is not auricular confession to a priest but reciprocal community confession and intercession. ὅπως ἰαθῆτε (so that you may be healed) — ἰάομαι is the standard healing verb, broader than just physical recovery. πολὺ ἰσχύει δέησις δικαίου ἐνεργουμένη — the grammar is contested: ἐνεργουμένη (being-energized) could be middle (prayer that is energized/operative) or passive (prayer that is set in motion by the Spirit); most interpreters take it as middle — 'the prayer of a righteous person, as it operates/in its working, is very powerful.'
17: Ἠλίας ἄνθρωπος ἦν ὁμοιοπαθὴς ἡμῖν — ὁμοιοπαθής (of like passions/nature — sharing the same susceptibilities as us) appears only here and Acts 14:15 in the NT; in Acts, Paul and Barnabas use it to deny their own divinity. James uses it to establish the full humanity of Elijah and therefore the relevance of his prayer as a model: his effectiveness in prayer is not due to some superhuman quality but to his righteousness (James 5:16) and earnestness. καὶ προσευχῇ προσηύξατο (and he prayed with prayer — a Hebraism, intensifying the earnestness) — cf. 1 Kings 17:1 (Elijah's prayer for drought) and 1 Kings 18:42-45 (the prayer for rain), both involving physical posture (1 Kings 18:42: he bowed himself down on the earth and put his face between his knees).
18: καὶ πάλιν προσηύξατο, καὶ ὁ οὐρανὸς ὑετὸν ἔδωκεν καὶ ἡ γῆ ἐβλάστησεν τὸν καρπὸν αὐτῆς — the brevity of the verse maps the structure of Elijah's prayer over seven petitions in 1 Kings 18:43-44 (the servant was sent seven times to look toward the sea before the cloud appeared). The cosmic scale of the answer — heaven gives rain, the earth brings forth her fruit — frames intercessory prayer as a participation in the ordering of creation. James's two examples (the drought and the rain) bracket the full range of prophetic intercession: both withholding and restoring the created order's blessing are within the scope of righteous prayer.
19: Ἀδελφοί μου, ἐάν τις ἐν ὑμῖν πλανηθῇ ἀπὸ τῆς ἀληθείας καὶ ἐπιστρέψῃ τις αὐτόν — the final pericope pivots from prayer for the sick to the ministry of doctrinal/ethical restoration. πλανηθῇ ἀπὸ τῆς ἀληθείας (stray from the truth, aorist passive subjunctive — the passive suggesting that wandering is something that happens to a person, not necessarily chosen deliberately) — ἀλήθεια in James encompasses both right belief and right conduct (James 1:18: 'he chose to give us birth through the word of truth'; James 3:14: do not boast and lie against the truth). ἐπιστρέψῃ τις αὐτόν (someone turns him back) — ἐπιστρέφω is the standard LXX and NT verb for repentance as 'turning' (Isaiah 6:10; Acts 3:19; Luke 1:16-17).
20: γινωσκέτω ὅτι ὁ ἐπιστρέψας ἁμαρτωλὸν ἐκ πλάνης ὁδοῦ αὐτοῦ σώσει ψυχὴν ἐκ θανάτου καὶ καλύψει πλῆθος ἁμαρτιῶν — σώσει ψυχὴν ἐκ θανάτου (will save a soul from death) — the subject is ambiguous: is it the intervenor who saves, or God through the intervenor? The Greek allows both; the agency of restoration points ultimately to divine mercy while crediting the human act. καλύψει πλῆθος ἁμαρτιῶν (will cover a multitude of sins) — from Proverbs 10:12 LXX (πάντας δὲ τοὺς μὴ φιλονεικοῦντας καλύπτει φιλία); 1 Peter 4:8 cites the same maxim. The whose sins are covered is debated — the wanderer's (the restored person) or the restorer's (as a merit earned). The most natural reading: the sins of the wanderer are covered through their restoration. The letter ends not with a doxology but with a human act of mercy — characteristically Jacobean in its fusion of faith and works.
